0.1: Statement of Compliance to Approved Training Organisation Certificate

The Organization Management Manual (OMM) has been thoroughly crafted to fully comply with the requirements outlined in the Approved Training Organisation Certificate (ATO). It incorporates the relevant Annexes to the TCAR PEL Regulation, as well as the applicable Acceptable Means of Compliance (AMC) and Guidance Material (GM). This manual faithfully represents the current company policies, regulations, and procedures. All operational personnel must strictly follow the policies, regulations, and procedures detailed in this manual. The Organisation Management Manual (OMM) encompasses the essential aspects of record keeping, quality, and safety. These elements are crucial for the certification of training courses, as outlined in the attachment to the Approved Training Organisation Certificate.
0.2: ATO (Approved Training Organisation):An aviation training institution that has been approved by the relevant regulatory authority, such as the Civil Aviation Authority of Thailand (CAAT), to conduct pilot and aviation personnel training in compliance with specified standards. CAAT (Civil Aviation Authority of Thailand): The regulatory body responsible for overseeing aviation safety and ensuring compliance with international and national aviation standards within Thailand. Proficiency Check: A flight or theoretical evaluation conducted to ensure that an individual possesses the necessary skills and knowledge to continue operating as a licensed pilot or instructor. Flight Instructor (FI): An individual who is licensed to provide flight instruction to student pilots, ensuring they are competent in both practical flying skills and aviation theory. 1: THE ORGANISATION AND SCOPE OF ACTIVITY
1.1.1: D-0507 commits to: Continuously Improve Safety Performance: We are committed to continuously enhancing our safety practices by learning from our operational experiences and implementing improvements. Safety performance is regularly monitored and assessed to ensure that we adapt to new challenges and industry best practices, promoting a safer training environment. Promote a Positive Safety Culture: D-0507 fosters a positive safety culture, where safety is a key consideration in all decisions made by staff, instructors, and students. Open communication and mutual respect are encouraged, ensuring that safety concerns are promptly reported and addressed. We strive to create an environment where everyone feels empowered to contribute to the organisation’s safety objectives. Compliance with Regulations: We are fully committed to complying with all applicable aviation regulations, including those set by the Civil Aviation Authority of Thailand (CAAT) and other relevant bodies. Our operations are designed to meet or exceed the safety standards established by regulatory authorities, ensuring full compliance in every aspect of training and operational activities. Provision of Resources: D-05
1.1.2: D-0507 Flight Training adheres to Just Culture principles, which ensure that our staff are treated fairly and encouraged to report safety concerns. We differentiate between acceptable and unacceptable behaviours as follows: Acceptable Behaviours: Actions that comply with training, procedures, and safety practices, even if they result in a reportable event or near-miss. Unacceptable Behaviours: Wilful disregard for safety protocols, negligent actions leading to safety risks, or malicious activities. Staff involved in incidents resulting from acceptable behaviours will receive support and protection from punitive actions. Incidents arising from unacceptable behaviours will be addressed through corrective actions aimed at preventing recurrence, not punishment. Safety Reporting Principles D-0507 encourage all personnel to report safety issues, concerns, or incidents. Reports can be made anonymously and will be handled in a confidential manner. Every report is seen as an opportunity to improve our safety systems and is essential for our ongoing safety assessments.
1.1.3: To ensure the effectiveness of this Organisation Management Manual, regular training sessions and meetings will be held to review and update procedures, ensuring all personnel remain informed and compliant with the manual’s guidelines. Routine audits and inspections will be conducted to assess the implementation of the policies and procedures outlined in the manual. The feedback and findings from these activities will be used to make continual improvements, ensuring that the management system evolves in line with regulatory requirements and operational needs. This proactive approach guarantees that all processes remain aligned with the safety and performance standards of the flight school.
1.2.1: At D0507, we are committed to maintaining the highest standards of quality, safety, and regulatory compliance in all aspects of our operations. This Compliance Policy Statement outlines our approach to quality control, auditing, and training, ensuring that all activities within the organisation align with the relevant aviation regulations and best practices.
1.2.2: We are dedicated to establishing a robust Quality Control System that monitors all operational and training processes within the flight school. This system ensures that every aspect of flight training, maintenance, and operational support meets the required safety and quality standards. Key elements of our quality control efforts include: Continuous Monitoring: Regular assessments of operational practices to ensure compliance with the Civil Aviation Authority of Thailand (CAAT) regulations, as well as other applicable international standards. Feedback Integration: Gathering input from students, instructors, and staff to make informed improvements to processes and procedures.
1.2.3: Regular Quality Audits are conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of our policies and procedures. These audits are essential for identifying non-conformances, potential risks, and areas for improvement. Our approach to auditing includes: Internal Audits: Scheduled internal audits to review compliance with training standards, safety procedures, and maintenance practices. All findings are documented and reviewed by the Compliance Monitoring Department. External Audits: Collaboration with external auditors and aviation authorities to verify that our operations align with industry standards. Corrective Action Plans: Upon identifying deficiencies or non-conformances, a Corrective Action Plan will be implemented. This plan outlines the steps required to rectify issues, prevent recurrence, and continuously enhance the quality of our operations.Training of Quality Personnel To maintain a culture of quality and compliance, D0507  is committed to the ongoing training of all personnel involved in quality control and assurance. 1.2.4: D0507 remains dedicated to fostering a culture of quality, safety, and compliance throughout the organisation. Our commitment to regular auditing, corrective action, and ongoing training ensures that we continue to meet the highest standards in the aviation industry. This Compliance Policy Statement reflects our ongoing dedication to improving the quality of our services and ensuring the safety and success of our students, instructors, and staff.

This Approved Training Organisation Management Manual (ATO-OMM) for D0507 outlines the quality, safety, and compliance systems that govern the organisation's operations. The manual encompasses the philosophies, policies, processes, guidelines, and responsibilities essential for maintaining a high standard of training and operational excellence. It includes detailed procedures for Safety and Compliance Management, ensuring that all aspects of the organisation align with industry best practices and regulatory standards. The ATO-OMM has been developed in accordance with the Civil Aviation Authority of Thailand (CAAT) Regulations, including relevant Acceptable Means of Compliance (AMC) and Guidance Material (GM). These regulations form the foundation for the organisation’s commitment to safety and compliance, as detailed in Chapter 1.6: Relevant Standards and Requirements. By adhering to these guidelines, this manual provides a comprehensive framework to support the continuous improvement of training operations, while fostering a culture of safety and regulatory compliance within the organisation.
1.5: This Approved Training Organisation (ATO) Certificate defines the scope of activities carried out by D0507 under the approval of the Civil Aviation Authority of Thailand (CAAT). The training provided under this certificate includes a comprehensive range of aviation-related courses, designed to meet the regulatory standards and operational needs of the aviation industry. The scope covers the following activities: Flight Training: Courses ranging from Private Pilot License (PPL) and other qualifications training. Theoretical Knowledge Instruction: A structured ground school program that includes subjects such as navigation, meteorology, air law, and flight performance. Ratings and Refresher Training: Specific training for aircraft types and recurrent training programs designed to maintain pilot proficiency and regulatory compliance. Safety and Compliance Management: Implementation of systems that ensure safety practices and adherence to compliance regulations across all training operations. Flight instruction in one's own aircraft: Providing flight instruction in a client's own aircraft with a D0507 instructor.
1.5.1: In addition to the approved training courses under the ATO certificate, D0507 also conducts non-training flight operations as defined in the Operations Manual (OM Part A). These operations include, but are not limited to, aircraft flight tests, non-revenue flights, company use flights, and any other flights concerning the company. Certain aircraft within the fleet, such as the Cessna T206H, are specifically designated for these company use operations. Note regarding Diamond DA42 (Pending Registration): The Diamond DA42 aircraft is currently in the process of registration. Once fully registered and officially endorsed in the ATO Training Specification, it will be utilized for both Multi-Engine Piston (MEP) training courses and designated company use flights. 3: Organisational Structure, Duties, Responsibilities and Accountabilities
3.1: The Head of Training (HT) is head of the D0507 For Flying Training School and has overall functional authority and responsibility for the effective and efficient running and management of the school. He also holds the Accountable Manager (ACM) position for the overall company function. Chief Flight Instructor (CFI) / Chief Theoretical Knowledge Instructor (CTKI) training and he reports to the HT to run and manage ground and flight training in accordance with ground and flight syllabus of the school. Compliance Monitoring Manager (CMM) reports to the ACM and HT in all aspects the management & administration, ground and flight training. [AKA Quality Management Manager: QAM] Administration and Financial (Admin) Services reports to the Accountable Manager in all aspect of finance, administration and human resource. Safety Manager (SM) is responsible for safety and security of overall organization report directly to ACM. Maintenance Manager (MM) is responsible in all aspect of maintenance of all training equipment.
3.3.1: The Accountable Manager (ACM), in line with the directives and policies of D0507, is responsible for overseeing the management and operations of the ATO certificate, as well as its administrative and supporting activities. This includes areas such as Maintenance Systems, Flight Operations, Ground and Flight Training, Marketing, Finance, and Corporate Communications. The ACM ensures that these infrastructures conform to the state's regulatory requirements and is ultimately responsible for the implementation and maintenance of the Safety Management System (SMS) throughout the entire company.
3.3.2: The Safety Management Manager, acting as the CAAT point of contact, is responsible for the development, administration, and maintenance of an effective Safety Management System (SMS). With delegated authority from the Accountable Manager (ACM), this role designs, plans, and coordinates with all departments to ensure compliance with industry guidelines and CAAT regulations. Additionally, the manager oversees the Emergency Response Program, Internal Evaluation Program, and SMS data utilized by the safety program.
3.3.3: The Compliance Monitoring Manager (CMM) is responsible for ensuring that the organization's activities comply with applicable regulatory requirements and standards, as well as any additional requirements established by the organization. The CMM oversees the management and administration of the Compliance Monitoring and Flight Training and Ground Training programs.
3.3.4: The Head of Training (HT) manages and supervises the progress and completion of training within the organization and reports to the Accountable Manager. The HT ensures that all training activities comply with regulatory requirements and internal standards, integrating flight training, flight simulation training devices (FSTD), and theoretical knowledge instruction. [N/A — D-0507 does not operate any FSTD.]
3.3.6: The Maintenance Manager (MM) reports to the Head of Training (HT) and is responsible for overseeing the aircraft maintenance performed by Contract Maintenance Providers, monitoring the airworthiness and day-to-day operation of the fleet, and ensuring compliance with CAAT-approved procedures.
3.3.7: The Chief Flight Instructor (CFI) is directly responsible to the Head of Training (HT) for all aspects of flight training. The CFI manages and supervises the planning and implementation of flight training activities, ensuring they are conducted effectively, efficiently, and safely. In accordance with ORA.ATO.210, the CFI is responsible for the supervision of all flight and flight simulation training instructors, and for the standardisation of all flight instruction and flight simulation instruction within the organisation.
3.3.8: The Chief Theoretical Knowledge Instructor (CTKI) is directly responsible to the Head of Training (HT) for all aspects of theoretical knowledge instruction. 4.6: The hazard identification process is the formal means of collecting, evaluating and recording hazards, evaluate the associated risk and define related mitigation measures.
4.6.1: Hazard: A hazard refers to any potential source that can cause damage, harm, or undesirable effects to anything or someone in a work environment, given specific conditions. Dangers can manifest in several forms, including physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, or psychosocial hazards. They are indicative of circumstances or procedures that possess the capacity to result in harm, sickness, or destruction. Risk: Risk is the likelihood or probability that a person will be harmed or experience an adverse health effect if exposed to a hazard. It also includes the severity of the harm or the extent of the damage that can result from exposure to the hazard. Essentially, risk is a combination of the likelihood of the occurrence of a hazardous event and the severity of the consequences.
4.6.2: The hazard identification process is a systematic approach to recognizing and documenting potential hazards within a ATO environment. This section outlines the steps involved in identifying hazards effectively.
4.6.3: Hazards could come from different sources, including: Natural and environmental: weather, earthquake, wind and sand, seawater, rocks, cliffs, ice structures, rough waters, volcano lava and dust, etc. Economic: competition, production pressure, cost pressure, etc. Unsafe conditions: use of unofficial documentation or documents not up to date, inadequate resources. Unsafe acts: errors, violations, negligence, sabotage, excessive/uncontrolled performance variations. Physiological: diseases, hypoxia, perceptual illusions, fatigue, sleep deprivation, jet lag, medication, alcohol, intoxication, digestion troubles, etc. Technological: design or maintenance related, hazardous material, pollution, explosions, etc. Operational or specific mission: obstacles, cables, demanding landing sites (moving platforms/off airfield sites), degraded visual cue (brown-out and white-out), demanding/overbearing customers, etc.
4.6.4: Risk cannot be eliminated; thus, the implementation of risk management processes is critical to an effective SMS. Hazard identification is the first step in the risk management process. The three methodologies for identifying hazards are: Reactive – Hazards are identified through investigation and analysis of past safety occurrences. Incidents and accidents are indicators of system deficiencies and can be used to determine the hazards that were contributing to the event or are latent. Proactive – Analysis of existing or real time situations. This is the safety assurance function's primary job with its audits, evaluations, employee reporting, and the associated analysis and assessment processes. This involves actively seeking hazards in the existing processes. Predictive – Data gathering in order to identify possible negative future outcomes or events. Analyzing system processes and the environment to identify potential future hazards and initiating mitigating actions. Hazards are identified from different internal and external sources:
4.6.5: Hazards are distinguished from hazard consequences. Consequences can be described based on the hazard information specifying the place, time, extent, nature, etc., of the event as required. Hazard identification also provides a systematic overview of all possible consequences. The following question should be asked for each hazard: What were or could have been the possible hazard consequences? Where information on the consequence of a hazard has been identified for a specific type of activity, it should be available directly from data sources (e.g. from reported accidents and occurrences or results of analysis already documented); such available information should be considered. It is also worth noting that the absence of past incidents/accidents does not mean the absence of risk. It is important, therefore, to identify the underlying hazards and to assess the risks. One effective way of doing this is to group similar events to try and identify the underlying hazards.
4.6.6: Hazards are identified through the application of a hazard identification process. The hazard identification process is the formal means of collecting, recording, analyzing, acting on, and generating feedback about hazards and the associated risks that affect D-0507’s operational activities' safety. The hazards identification process features several components: D-0507 Flight Training adopts the proactive approach of analyzing the conduct of operations to identify potential hazards, assess the associated risks, and then mitigate risk factors before they result in an accident or incident. This approach should trigger the following questions: What accidents or incidents could happen and why? For what reasons could these occur? Do we feel enough protected? Any action we should take now to prevent these from occurring? The proactive approaches are very effective tools for the management of safety, they should build upon solid reactive processes.

4.9: Risk mitigation strategies and Mitigation measures

The risk evaluation forms the basis for deciding on risk control (mitigating) measures and assessing these measures' effectiveness. Risk control measures identify the consequences associated with both an unacceptable risk and tolerable risk and where further risk reduction measures are feasible and reasonable. Identification of possible mitigation is based on the risk description and evaluation, considering in particular any uncertainties identified and critical assumptions made. Controls that may eliminate the consequence of a hazard, likelihood-reducing measures and severity-reducing measures are identified. The measures should address the human factors (e.g. training and competence), equipment or organizational factors (e.g. procedures). In the Company, the personnel contribute to the definition of risk control measures where they concern personal equipment (goggles, helmets and other flight equipment), by their acceptance and use.
4.9.1: The Risk Mitigation Process ensures that identified risks are appropriately addressed to reduce or eliminate their impact on operations. This process is part of the organisation's Safety Management System (SMS) and follows the analysis and assessment of risks through the risk assessment process. The mitigation process involves determining the necessary actions to control risks to an acceptable level, ensuring the safety and continuity of operations. The following outlines the key steps: 1. Risk Identification and Prioritisation After the risk assessment, risks are classified based on their probability and severity. Priority is given to risks rated as "unacceptable" or "tolerable with mitigation" as identified through the organisation's Risk Matrix (refer to SMS Chapter 4.2.5, "Tolerability Matrix"). 2. Development of Mitigation Strategies Mitigation Measures: Based on the identified risk, specific mitigation actions are developed to reduce the likelihood of the event occurring or lessen its impact. 5.6: Implementing an effective corrective or preventive action capable of satisfying quality assurance and regulatory documentation requirements is accomplished in seven basic steps: The Identification of the problem, nonconformity, incident or the potential problem, nonconformity, or incident. An Evaluation of the magnitude of the problem and potential impact on the company. The development of an Investigation procedure with assignments of responsibility. Performing a thorough analysis of the problem with appropriate documentation Creating an Action Plan listing all the tasks required to correct and/or prevent the problem. The Implementation of the plan. A thorough Follow up with verification of the completion of all tasks, and an assessment of the appropriateness and effectiveness of the actions taken
5.6.1: The initial step in the process is to define the problem clearly. It is important to accurately and completely describe the situation as it exists now. This should include the source of information, a detailed explanation of the problem, the available evidence that a problem exists.
5.6.2: The specific origin of the information that initiated this action is recorded. Documenting the information source can be very useful when conducting an investigation into the problem and implementing the created action plan. It will also provide data for evaluating the quality system's effectiveness and facilitating communicating the action's completion to the appropriate individuals or departments. This information may come from many possible sources. For example, situations that require corrective actions may come from external sources such as customer concerns or service requests. Internal quality audits, staff observations, quality assurance inspections, trending data, and management review are examples of possible internal sources of information. Examples of sources that lead to preventive actions may include: Occurrence report Internal Quality Audit Feed Back Compliance Monitoring Inspection Staff Observation Financial Report Risk Assessment Process Performance Monitoring Management Review Other sources are possible and will depend on the circumstances. This procedure must be able to handle both internal findings and external findings (e.g., findings made by CAAT or a third p
5.6.3: The situation described and documented in the “Identification” section should now be evaluated to determine first, the need for action and then the level of action required. The potential impact of the problem and the actual risks to the company and/or customers must be determined. Essentially, the reason that this problem is a concern must be documented.
5.6.4: The investigation procedure that was created is now used to investigate the cause of the problem. This analysis aims to determine the root cause of the problem described, but any contributing causes are also identified. This process involves collecting relevant data, investigating all possible causes, and using the information available to determine the problem's cause. It is very important to distinguish between the observed symptoms of a problem and the fundamental (root) cause.
5.6.5: Initiation of actions and measures (Action Plan)

Using the analysis results, the optimum method for correcting the situation (or preventing a future occurrence) is determined and an action plan developed. The plan should include, as appropriate: the items to be completed, document changes, any process, procedure, or system changes required, employee training, and any monitors or controls necessary to prevent the problem or a recurrence of the problem. The action plan should also identify the person or persons responsible for completing each task.
5.6.6: The corrective / preventive action plan that has been created is now implemented. All of the required tasks listed and described in the action plan are initiated, completed, and documented.
5.6.7: One of the most fundamental steps in the CAPA process is evaluating the actions taken. Several key questions must be answered: Have all of the objectives of this CAPA been met? (Did the actions correct or prevent the problem and are there assurances that the same situation will not happen again?) Have all recommended changes been completed and verified. Has appropriate communications and training been implemented to assure that all relevant employees understand the situation and the changes that have been made? Is there any chance that the actions taken may have had any additional adverse effect on the product or service?

9: The investigation tends to find the root cause of the incident and/or accident occurred. The purpose of accident investigation is to establish prevention, including the gathering and analysis of information, the drawing of conclusions, including the determination of causes and, when appropriate, the making of safety recommendations. [ICAO Annex 13] The sole objective of investigating an accident or incident shall be the prevention of accidents and incidents. It is not the purpose of this activity to apportion blame or liability. It should be emphasized that investigations are focused on the facts of an occurrence, identify the causal factors underlying the occurrence, and facilitate the development of agreed actions to prevent recurrence. Active failures and local factors need to be identified. However, from the perspective of safety enhancement, it is more important to identify systemic weakness such as inadequate defenses and organizational factors. Investigations will be conducted in a Just Culture environment.
9.1: Set up of the investigation team with the required skills and expertise. The size of the team and the expert profile of its members depend on the nature and severity of the occurrence being investigated. The investigating team may require the assistance of other specialists. Often, a single person is assigned to carry out internal (to the concerned organisation) investigation of an incident considered to have limited potential to cause harm. Gathering of factual information that is pertinent to the understanding of the circumstances and the events leading to the occurrence. A variety of information sources will be used to collect the necessary data for the reconstruction of the event. To ensure the continued availability of such data for the purpose of aviation safety improvement, information sources need to be protected. Guidance on the protection of safety information sources used for investigations is provided by ICAO Doc 9859 - Safety management manual, Third Edition 2012. Event reconstruction in order to establish the exact sequence of events leading to the safety occurrence with its causal and contributory factors. The reconstruction phase's output should be a set of events t

9.2.2: The occurrence related flight safety which does not qualify for a level 1 investigation but cannot otherwise be fully explained without a thorough investigation shall be subject to a level 2 investigation

9.4: All Staff – mean all level staff within a Just Culture environment, assist the investigation activity in developing Findings and Actions that will prevent recurrence of a similar event by providing honest and accurate information to Investigators. Working with Interested Parties to develop achievable safety actions that will address the findings made. 9.4.1: An investigation procedure and format includes the integration of safety related findings with the SMS.

Internal safety investigations are not intended to be accusatory or punitive. However, the investigation may highlight or reveal unsafe acts, errors or mistakes by an individual, the organization, a service provider or the regulatory authority. In such cases the Lead Investigator has a responsibility to ensure that all such matters are identified and reported. It should be emphasized that investigations are focused on the facts of an occurrence, identify the causal factors underlying the occurrence, and facilitate the development of agreed actions to prevent recurrence. Active failures and local factors need to be identified. However, from the perspective of safety enhancement, it is more important to identify systemic weakness such as inadequate defenses and organizational factors. The principle questions that the Lead Investigator will seek to answer are, “What is this occurrence telling us about the system?”, “What safety deficiencies exist in the system and why?”, “What safety action should be recommended to reduce or eliminate these deficiencies and make the airline’s operation safer?” D-0507 will assess the benefits and challenges of integrating management system processes ac
9.4.2: A level 1 investigation shall be performed in the same manner as guideline in ICAO Annex 13. SM shall interview the crew members involved and present to Investigation Committee for investigation. IM shall be invited to participate in interviewing the crew members. While the official Annex 13 investigation is conducted by the National Authority (AAIC/CAAT), the D0507 Level 1 Investigation Committee shall conduct an internal investigation to support the authority and identify immediate preventive actions. If the occurrence involves operational aspects, ACM shall be informed as soon as possible and decide whether or not the crew shall be released for active dutyIf the occurrence involves technical aspects, MM shall be informed as soon as possible and decide whether or not the airplane can be released back into service If the occurrence is of interest to the National Investigation Board, ACM shall establish contact with the Board.
9.4.3: The extent of the investigation shall be adapted to each actual occurrence at the discretion of the investigator. The investigation's main purpose is to establish the probable causes of the incidence and recommend remedial action to prevent recurrences. The investigation shall also support in establishing risk parameters. SM shall interview the crew members involved and present to investigation commitee for investigation. IM shall be invited to participate in interviewing the crew members. When deemed necessary, a combined investigation may be performed, where different functions take part.
9.4.4: An SMS follow up actions when investigations are addressed.

The result of investigation will be use as a topic for surveillance or audit to the related department by using the following four key elements: Safety performance monitoring can verify safety performance and validate the effectiveness of safety risk management. The safety survey is a cost-effective, easy-to-conduct and flexible method to allow the workforce to provide improvement proposals, and self-reflect and confirm their conformance with SMS requirements. Recommend improvements where needed to assure managers of the safety of activities within their areas and confirm conformance with applicable parts of their safety management systems. Audit and inspection activities should be undertaken to identify and eliminate any substandard performance of the SMS and safety operation, before it has an impact on safety. Documentation should be used to demonstrate activities performed or results achieved
9.4.5: Formal investigations will require a draft report to be distributed to interested parties for comments and feedback before the final report is released. Any factual errors or omissions that the stakeholders consider are evident in the draft report must be forwarded to the Lead Investigator within 14 days of the draft report release. The investigation team will review and agree on changes and safety actions will then be incorporated into the final report. The investigation report will be finalized once the safety actions are agreed with the relevant business unit. The final report will then be distributed to the relevant stakeholders. 9.4.8: The crash site should be cordoned off as soon as possible until the arrival of the investigation teams. Where possible, the Station Representative/Agents or officials should try to collect as much evidence as possible at the scene of the accident. 10.1: D0507’s system of record keeping allows the storage and reliable traceability of all its activities and related data. The record keeping system ensures that the records are always accessible and traceable throughout the required retention period and protection from damage, alteration and theft are ensured.
10.1.1: The Document Control and Management Lifecycle outlines the systematic process through which documents are created, managed, stored, and eventually archived or destroyed, ensuring the efficient and secure handling of all organisational records. This lifecycle is divided into three main stages: Data Collection, Record Management (Storage and Backup), and End of Storage Period.
10.1.2: The Documentation Control Procedure ensures that all organisational documents, including manuals, records, and reports, are properly managed, distributed, updated, and stored in compliance with regulatory requirements and internal policies. This procedure establishes clear guidelines for the creation, revision, approval, distribution, and archiving of documents to maintain consistency, accuracy, and traceability across the organisation.
10.2: Procedures for Security of Records, Security System, Document Control, and Accessibility

This section outlines the procedures for ensuring the security, confidentiality, and accessibility of all records and documents within the organisation. It details the methods used to manage, control, and protect important operational, training, and safety documentation, ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements and safeguarding sensitive information. The processes described herein ensure that records are properly maintained, accessed by authorised personnel only, and securely stored both physically and digitally.
10.2.1: The company takes all reasonable steps to protect the security of the personal information that it holds and maintain the accuracy and relevance of the data it holds. This includes appropriate measures to protect electronic materials and materials stored and generated in hard copy. All student / employee records (except assessment records) are to be confirmed in writing with each student/employee, and updated at least every six calendar months by the Admin, while the student continues to be enrolled or during employment. Where personal data held by the company is no longer required for the company’s administration purposes, and the retention is not required, then the company will destroy the personal information by a secure means. All student and employee records at the HQ are stored securely and kept confidential.
10.2.2: The records are kept by Administration department to facilitate the training of students and profiling of the employee. Instructor and related staff members may also keep informal records relating to their functional responsibilities with individual students for a time being. The company has a policy on Confidentiality of Records. The company has also developed related policies that concentrate on the use of data and computer and network security. The company is committed to maintaining the privacy and confidentiality of information regarding students/employee. The information is requested at the time of licensing or endorsement course application to the company, but is used in a very limited and controlled manner.
10.2.4: The company must protect student/employee personal information by making reasonable security arrangements against such risks as unauthorized access, collection, use, disclosure or destruction. In the event that the company ceases to operate, arrangements will be made for access to current student records including: A parchment for each qualification completed; For students/ employees who haven’t completed their qualification, a statement of attainment for any units of competence or modules completed; and Any training and assessment activities undertaken that were only in partial fulfilment of a unit of competence or module. Students that continue their studies with another provider will generally have their records transferred to their new provider with their written consent. Otherwise, the arrangements will be made for records to be held by an authorized representative organization on behalf of the company (such as a new provider).
10.3.1: Procedures to keep records of Student

To provide an accurate, timely, relevant student record that facilitates the delivery of safe and coordinated practice that involves the student and the instructor. All Instructor and administrative staff creating or contributing to the student record will provide an accurate and timely record which can determine accountability; facilitate training decision making; improve training service through clear communication of the assessment and progress planning rationale; provide a consistent approach to partnership working; and help in the investigation of complaints or legal proceedings. The eligible students also have certain rights with respect to their student records. These rights include: The right to inspect and review the student's education records. The right to request the amendment of the student's education records that the student believes is inaccurate, misleading, or otherwise in violation of the student's privacy rights. The right to provide written consent before the company discloses personally identifiable information (as mentioned above) from the student's training records, except emergency condition and concerning misdemeanor.
10.3.2: Student records are defined as those records directly related to a student and maintained by the company or by a party acting for the company.  Included are theoretical records and student practice records. Counseling, medical Certification, license and student privileges records are the part of student records and, further, education records but do not include: Records that are kept in the sole possession of the maker, are used only as a personal memory aid, and are not accessible or revealed to any other person except a temporary substitute for the maker of the record. Records of the law enforcement unit of an educational agency or institution. Records related to an individual who is employed by an agency or other institution, that: are made and maintained in the normal course of business; related exclusively to the individual in that individual's capacity as an employee; and are not available for use for any other purpose. Records on a student, that are: made or maintained by a physician, psychiatrist, psychologist, or other recognized professional or paraprofessional acting in his or her professional capacity or assisting in a paraprofessional capacity; made, maintained, or use
10.3.3: A student should submit to the Admin or other appropriate official, a written request that identifies the record(s) the student wishes to inspect. 10.3.14: Electronic records are records maintained in a format that requires the use of technology to access. Electronic records may take many forms including, without limitation, e-mail, text messaging, SMS, word processing documents, spreadsheets, web content, databases, scanned images. Electronic records may be maintained in an electronic form that permits reasonable retrieval and access for the length of the prescribed retention period. Electronic record systems may require additional planning and maintenance to ensure that the systems and technology used to create, maintain, access, retrieve and dispose of the records remains functional throughout the length of the record’s retention period. Electronic records management should include consideration regarding the ability to dispose of records at the end of their retention period. Additional considerations for electronic record systems include identifying security measures to ensure that the records maintained are accurate, authentic, free from unauthorized access or alterations and that disaster recovery plans exist.
10.3.15: Email is an example of an electronic record format. Employees are responsible to retain and dispose of email that they send and receive in carrying out their employment responsibilities. 10.5.2: Disposal is an important part of records management. Properly done, it ensures that the company retains records for as long as they are needed and then, when they are no longer needed, destroys them in an appropriate manner or disposes of them in some other way, e.g. by transfer to an archives service. A managed disposal process has several benefits: it avoids unnecessary storage costs incurred by using office or server space to maintain records no longer needed by the company it supports compliance with the 5th data protection principle if records contain personal information (this principle requires company not to keep personal information for longer than necessary) finding and retrieving information is quicker and easier because there is less to search Unless prohibited from doing so for legal purposes, records should be disposed of as follows: Records included in the Retention Schedule: Responsible departments shall destroy records at the end of the records’ retention period as prescribed by the Retention Schedule. Copies of records that are made by the Responsible Offices for their own convenience purposes should be destroyed no later than and in the same manner as the “master
10.5.3: The company shall ensure that all destruction of records is conducted in a manner that safeguards the interests of the safety, security, and privacy of personnel. In destroying records containing information that is confidential or exempt from disclosure, company shall employ destruction methods that prevent unauthorized access to or use of the information and ensure that the information cannot practicably be read, reconstructed, or recovered. The agency shall specify the manner of destruction of such records when documenting disposition. Where possible, recycling following destruction is encouraged. For hard copy records containing information that is confidential or exempt from disclosure, appropriate destruction methods include burning in an industrial incineration facility, pulping, pulverizing, shredding, or macerating. High wet strength paper, paper mylar, durable-medium paper substitute, or similar water repellent papers are not sufficiently destroyed by pulping and require other methods such as shredding or burning. 10.5.4: THIRD PARTY SERVICE PROVIDERS for DISPOSAL

Considerations When Outsourcing Disposal of Paper Documents and Other Physical Media When disposal of paper documents is outsourced, the accountability and responsibility to ensure that the personal data on such paper documents are destroyed remains with the company. Therefore, where disposal is outsourced, the company should ensure that contracts with third party service providers contain the necessary terms and conditions to comply with the obligations under the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA). This includes having such third party service providers take reasonable measures to protect the personal data on the paper from disclosure to unauthorized parties during the entire disposal process. To do so, company would need to understand how these third party service providers dispose of the paper, including the supply chain arrangements.
10.5.5: Summary of points to consider when outsourcing disposal:

Assess the suitability of the services for the kind and volume for disposal; for mass disposal of documents in files and with clips etc., ensure that the disposal service permits metal or plastic pieces; Assess the service provider’s overall processes and protection during transport, storage, and actual destruction; it may be more difficult to assess cases where only the collection is done in Thailand, but actual destruction occurs at an overseas location; Assess whether containers are locked or secured during transit, whether policies for accident and incident reporting are in place, and whether the shredding /incineration /pulping facility has physical security in place; Keep records of collection and destruction confirmation. Some service providers may be certified or accredited, and may be able to provide a formal certificate of destruction; otherwise maintain internal records when these matters have been sent for disposal; Collection (or handover) of waste items (e.g. paper documents) should be supervised and documented; the waste items should not be stored unsecured for easy collection by the outsourced party;

11.2: For comprehensive policies, definitions, and procedures regarding the leasing of aeroplanes, refer to OM Part A, Chapter A.25 (LEASING). The organisation recognises the following definitions for leasing agreements: Dry Lease: Is when the aeroplane is operated under the ATO or authorization of the lessee. Wet Lease: Is when the aeroplane is operated under the ATO or authorization of the lessor.
11.2.1: Dry Lease-in: A dry lease-in must be approved by the CAAT. The Accountable Manager (ACM) shall ensure that any conditions of this approval are included in the lease agreement, and any differences from the requirements are notified to and acceptable to the CAAT. Wet Lease-in: A wet lease-in must be approved by the CAAT. The ACM shall organise an audit to ensure that the lessor's safety and maintenance standards are equivalent to Thai ANA, the lessor holds an ATO from a Chicago Convention signatory state, the aeroplane has a standard Certificate of Airworthiness, and all applicable Authority requirements are complied with.
11.2.2: Dry Lease-out: A dry lease-out for training purposes to another operator may be achieved provided the aeroplane is maintained according to an approved maintenance programme, and the Authority has exempted the lessor from relevant CAAT provisions and removed the aeroplane from its ATO. Wet Lease-out: If the organisation provides an aeroplane and complete crew to another entity while retaining all functions and responsibilities prescribed by CAAT, the organisation shall remain the operator of the aeroplane.

0.1: STATEMENT OF COMPLIANCE TO APPROVED TRAINING ORGANISATION CERTIFICATE

The Operation Manual (OM) has been thoroughly crafted to fully comply with the requirements outlined in D0507. It incorporates the relevant Annexes to the TCAR PEL Regulation, as well as the applicable Acceptable Means of Compliance (AMC) and Guidance Material (GM). For additional information, please refer to OMM, Chapter 1.6, titled "Relevant Standards and Requirements." This manual faithfully represents the current company policies, regulations, and procedures. All operational personnel must strictly follow the policies, regulations, and procedures detailed in this manual. The Operation Manual (OM) is related to the training course approval, as specified in the attachment to the Approved Training Organisation Certificate.
0.2.1: APPROVED: Officially accepted as being correct or satisfactory by CAAT. APPROVED DATE: The date which the document is approved by the CAAT or Head of Department (company approved document). AUTHORITY: Means the Civil Aviation Authority of Thailand (CAAT). AUTHORITY (REGULATORY): A government agency or other administrative body that exercises regulatory or oversight control over operations or activities within a defined jurisdiction. Take-off Alternate means an alternate aerodrome at which an aircraft would be able to land should this become necessary shortly after take-off and it is not possible to use the aerodrome of departure. En-Route Alternate means an alternate aerodrome at which an aircraft would be able to land in the event that a diversion becomes necessary while en-route. Destination Alternate means an alternate aerodrome at which an aircraft would be able to land should it become either impossible or inadvisable to land at the aerodrome of intended landing. A.4.6: AUTHORIZATION AND OPERATIONAL COMPLIANCE FOR OWNER’S AIRCRAFT

For flight instruction conducted in a client's own aircraft (as defined in A.4.5), specific operational protocols must be strictly adhered to ensure safety and compliance during the aircraft's stay with D0507. The authorizing personnel and the Pilot-in-Command (PIC) must verify the following conditions prior to flight:
1: The aircraft owner must formally authorise D0507 to use the aircraft for training purposes. As the aircraft is capable of transitioning between private use and training, it is incumbent upon the owner or the Pilot-in-Command (PIC) to explicitly specify the purpose of the flight as "Training" in the official flight plan.
2: Insurance Requirements: The aircraft owner is responsible for obtaining comprehensive insurance coverage for flight training use, including third-party liability coverage as per the legal and regulatory requirements of the CAAT. Airworthiness Compliance: The aircraft owner must ensure that the aircraft remains airworthy at all times by adhering to the manufacturer's maintenance manual and complying with CAAT regulations. All maintenance and inspections must be conducted by licensed and certified aircraft maintenance personnel. Updating and Exchanging Aircraft Documentation Important aircraft documents must be regularly updated and shared with D0507 electronically or via the designated online form. These include: Certificate of Airworthiness Aircraft Maintenance Log Inspection Reports (Annual/50, 100, 150, 200-hour inspections)
4: Journey Log Submission: The aircraft owner must submit the aircraft’s Journey Log to D0507 via the designated online form. This must be done for the last operating date prior to the flight training, the day of the training flight, and after any maintenance activity. The log must include recent operational hours of the engine and airframe, details of recent maintenance, replaced components, and reports of any malfunctions. Flight Risk Assessment (FRA): Pilots must conduct a Flight Risk Assessment (FRA) before every flight via the D0507 online system. The assessment must consider weather conditions, pilot limitations, aircraft readiness, and operational constraints such as destination airport conditions.
5: Safety Monitoring: The aircraft owner must monitor, analyze, and periodically share safety data with D0507 to assess risk trends and enhance the Safety Management System (SMS). Accident/Incident Reporting: In the event of an accident or incident involving a training aircraft, mandatory reporting to the relevant authorities, including CAAT, is required. The owner must also notify D0507 immediately via the official online safety reporting channel to facilitate immediate response measures.
6: Instructors and students must log flight hours according to their authorized privileges and CAAT regulations (e.g., logging as Dual, PIC, or Solo). Prior to any solo flight, instructors must complete a Solo Release Form and retain it for records

A.5.2: Cancel of Flight Procedure for Flight Operation

Purpose: The Cancel of Flight procedure is designed to ensure the proper handling and communication of flight cancellations within the flight school. This procedure aims to maintain operational efficiency, minimize disruption, and ensure that all relevant personnel and students are informed promptly when a flight is canceled. Since A pilot must be present at least 30 minutes before the scheduled flight, while a student pilot must be present at least 60 minutes before the scheduled flight to ensure proper preparation. This time is used for meeting with the instructor for a pre-flight briefing, reviewing weather conditions, completing necessary paperwork (such as flight planning and weight and balance calculations), conducting a supervised pre-flight aircraft inspection, and ensuring that all documentation is in order. Arriving early helps maintain time management, ensures the session starts promptly, and allows the student to make any necessary adjustments to the flight plan based on updated weather or notices. Failure to arrive on time can disrupt the schedule and compromise flight safety. Scope: This procedure applies to all flight operations, including student flights, training s

A.7: Crew composition and Command of Aircraft
PIC is the commander of the aircraft, in D0507 is define to instructor act as PIC in teaching flight, any company pilot act as PIC, or Student Act as PIC in training flight.
A.7.1: The minimum flight crew required for the operation of each aircraft type used by D-0507 Flight Training Co., Ltd. is specified in the relevant Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM) or Pilot’s Operating Handbook (POH). For all aircraft currently operated, the minimum flight crew is one (1) pilot. Note: While the minimum crew is one pilot, training flights will normally consist of a Student Pilot and a Flight Instructor.
A.7.2: The PIC is responsible for the safety of all on board when the doors are closed. They also ensure the aircraft’s operation and safety from takeoff to landing, shutting down the engines. The PIC must ensure all specified checklists are followed. Since the company provides flight pilots with an aircraft operating manual, the PIC ensures all procedures and checklists align with the manual, AFM, POH, etc., and good airmanship standards. The PIC must notify the nearest authority of any accidents involving the aircraft, resulting in serious injury or death, or substantial damage to the aircraft or property. The PIC must report all known or suspected defects in the aircraft to the operator at the flight’s end. The PIC is responsible for the aircraft log book and/or the general declaration containing the listed information.
A.7.3: The PIC shall: Have authority to give all commands he deems necessary for the purpose of securing the safety of the airplane and of persons or property carried therein; Have authority to justify that safety requirements such as the technical standard of the airplane, adequate pilot, weather, enroute, alternate aerodrome, minimum fuel , etc.,are satisfied before the flight plan for his flight is approved; Have authority and joint responsibility form a kind decision on operational control and flight dispatch regarding flight planning of his flight; Have authority to disembark any person, or any part of the cargo, which, in his opinion, may represent a potential hazard to the safety of the airplane; Have the right to refuse transportation of inadmissible personnel if their carriage poses any risk to the safety of the airplane or its occupants; Satisfy himself that the airplane is airworthy and its configuration and equipment are in accordance with the MEL and decide whether or not to accept the airplane with item unserviceable allowed by the MEL; Have authority to take extra fuel on top of the minimum fuel for his flight whenever he considers the weather and other conditions warrant;
A.7.4: Duties and Responsibilities of Crew Member other than the Pilot-in-Command

Aircraft operated by D-0507 Flight Training are single-pilot certified. The second crew seat is not mandatory, but the PIC can delegate specific duties to assist with safe flight conduct. The PIC decides the extent of delegation and doesn’t transfer command authority. A non-primary pilot is a passenger, except in flight training where an instructor is the PIC and a student receives dual instruction. If a second person is on board and assigned duties by the PIC, they may: Read checklists Monitor flight instruments and engine parameters Communicate with ATC Manage navigation systems Report abnormalities to the PIC They must perform only assigned duties and not handle controls, adjust systems, or communicate with ATC unless directed by the PIC. In training flights, the PIC (FI) can assign progressively greater responsibilities based on the student’s training stage and syllabus. This doesn’t change the PIC’s command authority or responsibility for flight safety. The PIC resumes direct control immediately if safety requires it, without prior notice. No one except the PIC can override, countermand, or interfere with the PIC’s decisions or actions, except where immediate safety requires a

A.13: Flying duty period and flight time limitations (flying instructor)

Refer to TCAR Part ORO, which complies with the Air Crew Regulation and that all company pilot must at a minimum adhere to. The subsequent definitions pertain to flying time, flight duty period, and applicable limitations: Break: A period of time within a flight duty period that is shorter than a rest period. During a break, a crew member is free of all tasks, although it counts as duty time. Duty: Any task performed by a crew member for the operator. This includes flight duties, administrative work, providing or receiving training, checking, positioning, and certain elements of standby. Duty Period: The time span starting when a crew member is required by the operator to report for or commence duty, and ending when the crew member is free from all duties, including post-flight responsibilities. Flight Duty Period (FDP): The period that begins when a crew member is required to report for duty and includes one or more flight sectors. It concludes when the aircraft comes to a final stop and the engines are shut down at the end of the last sector on which the crew member is serving as part of the operating crew. A.15: LIMITATIONS AND MINIMUM REST PERIOD FOR PILOT AND INSTRUCTOR
A.15.1: Minimum rest period at home base.

The minimum rest period provided before undertaking an FDP starting at home base shall be at least as long as the preceding duty period, or 12 hours, whichever is greater. By way of derogation from number 1., the minimum rest provided under “Minimum rest period away from home base.” applies if the operator provides suitable accommodation to the instructors at home base. Flight Instructors shall be provided with a recurrent extended recovery rest period to compensate for cumulative fatigue. The minimum recurrent extended recovery rest period shall be at least 36 hours, including 2 local nights. In any case, the time between the end of one recurrent extended recovery rest period and the start of the next extended recovery rest period shall not be more than 168 hours. The recurrent extended recovery rest period shall be increased to 2 local days twice every month. Flight instructor shall not perform continuously more than 3 nights of night flying duty without a rest period of at least 36 hours, including 2 local nights.
A.15.2: Minimum rest period away from home base.

Unexpected circumstances that require the pilot to stay away from home base or be assigned. The minimum rest period provided before undertaking an FDP starting away from home base shall be at least as long as the preceding duty period, or 10 hours, whichever is greater. This period shall include an 8-hour sleep opportunity in addition to the time for travelling and physiological needs.
A.16: LIMITATIONS AND MINIMUM REST PERIOD FOR Student

Students shall not start a training flight if they know that they are suffering from, or are likely to suffer from fatigue, or if they feel unfit to the extent that the flight may be endangered and/or the training target is in question.
A.16.1: Minimum rest period at home base.

The minimum rest period provided before undertaking an FDP starting at home base shall be at least as long as the preceding duty period, or 12 hours, whichever is greater. By way of derogation from number 1., the minimum rest provided under “Minimum rest period away from home base.” applies if the operator provides suitable accommodation to the student at home base. Student pilots shall be provided with a recurrent extended recovery rest period of at least 36 hours, including 2 local nights. A student pilot shall not perform continuously more than 6 days of flying duty, or more than 3 nights of night flying duty, without receiving this minimum 36-hour rest period.
A.16.2: Minimum rest period away from home base.

Unexpected circumstances that require the pilot to stay away from home base or be assigned. The minimum rest period provided before undertaking an FDP starting away from home base shall be at least as long as the preceding duty period, or 10 hours, whichever is greater. This period shall include an 8-hour sleep opportunity in addition to the time for travelling and physiological needs.

A.18.6: In preparation for any type of flight, the pilot or instructor and the trainee must meticulously gather and document crucial information to ensure a safe and efficient operation. This includes the collection of elevation and density details for each airport involved in their journey. Understanding these parameters is vital for navigating varying airport altitudes and atmospheric conditions, which can significantly impact aircraft performance. Additionally, they must carefully calculate the mass and balance of the aircraft. This calculation is essential for determining the weight distribution and center of gravity, which are critical for maintaining the aircraft's stability and control during flight. All this information must be thoroughly documented and readily accessible throughout the operation, serving as a vital reference for both the pilot and the trainee to ensure a successful and safe flight.
A.18.7: The pilot or the instructor and the trainee must be vigilant in gathering and analyzing critical information that could impact their flight. This includes understanding the minimum safe altitudes to maintain over varying terrain to ensure adequate clearance from obstacles. They must also determine the optimal cruising altitude for their specific flight plan, which involves considering factors such as aircraft performance, fuel efficiency, and airspace restrictions. Weather is another crucial consideration, as it affects visibility, icing conditions, and turbulence, all of which require careful attention to ensure flight safety. Lastly, the limitations of wind, especially during training flights, must be respected; this includes crosswind components for takeoff and landing, as well as gust factors that could affect aircraft control.
A.19: Ensuring a safe and secure operation is a collective duty that must be embraced by all. This involves strict compliance with the safety policy and adherence to the operational policies, procedures, and directives outlined in the organization's official documents. Utilizing established reporting systems and possessing a thorough understanding of detailed emergency response protocols are critical for maintaining operational safety and security. The objective of the organization's safety management is to uphold, and when possible, enhance safety standards across all operations while actively working to reduce the risk of an aircraft accident to the lowest feasible level. (see also Safety Management Manual) In addition to the management's role within the training organization, instructors play a pivotal role in upholding safety. Their commitment to safe practices, continuous promotion of safety as a fundamental component of daily training activities and leading by example in the operation of aircraft are indispensable in fostering a culture of safety.
A.19.1: The pilot or the instructor and the trainee are required to operate the aircraft in strict accordance with the Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM) or Pilot’s Operating Handbook (POH) and to follow manufacturer-provided operating instructions for specific equipment. They must ensure the equipment is used to its full and optimal capacity, handling it with care. All necessary instruments and equipment for conducting a flight must be installed and operational, following the guidelines outlined in the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the list of deficiencies, as well as manufacturer instructions and Airworthiness Directives (AD) from the manufacturer's country of origin, detailed in the Operations Manual, Part B. It's important to note that the MEL applies to aircraft during transit and layover, irrespective of its base location. If a defect arises when an aircraft is positioned at the terminal for pre-departure, it is considered a transit aircraft, and the MEL requirements are activated. However, the MEL does not apply to issues that arise during flight; these must be managed as per the Flight Manual's guidance. A.19.2: In accordance with the pre-flight procedure for the concerned aircraft, the emergency equipment is to be checked for availability and serviceability. Ensuring the presence and readiness of emergency equipment is a critical aspect of safety in training aircraft operations. Each piece of equipment must be routinely checked for its presence, accessibility, and operational status. Fire Extinguisher: This is a vital piece of equipment in the event of a fire. Its location should be clearly marked, and it must be easily accessible to both the instructor and trainee without obstruction. Regular checks are required to ensure the extinguisher is fully charged, as indicated by the gauge, and that its safety seal is intact. Emergency Escape Equipment: Items like an emergency safety hammer or belt cutter should be stowed in a designated, easily reachable spot. This equipment must be checked for proper functionality and should not be sealed so that it can be accessed quickly in an emergency situation. A.19.3: The Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT) is a critical piece of safety equipment in any aircraft, designed to signal distress in the event of an emergency. It is imperative to conduct periodic checks for accidental activations by tuning into frequencies 121.5 MHz or 406 MHz using the appropriate radio equipment. If an accidental activation is detected, the response should be swift and follow the guidelines provided by the manufacturer's operating manual. The general steps include resetting the ELT or turning it off, then returning it to the 'ARM' position to ensure it is ready for actual emergencies. For Personal Locator Beacons (PLBs), if provided, the transmitter should be turned off as per the instructions in the user manual. Following these actions, it is a standard procedure to inform the Bangkok Rescue Coordination Center (RCC) of the accidental activation to prevent any unnecessary search and rescue operations. It is crucial that all aircraft personnel are familiar with these procedures and adhere to them to maintain the integrity of the emergency response system.

A.22: Both the Air Safety Report (ASR) and the Mandatory Occurrence Report (MOR) shall be referred to together as MOR. The safety manager must receive all MORs within twenty-four hours of the incident being reported. The report should be transmitted via email to the designated channel without delay. The substance of reports must be shared in the interest of flight safety; this is a core principle of the Mandatory Occurrence Reporting (MOR) programme, which is mandated by law in the majority of the countries. In order to carry out its duties in a proper manner, the regulatory body shall not reveal the identity of the individual who submitted the report or any individual to whom it pertains, unless legally obligated to do so or with the consent of the affected party in either case. In the event that flight safety follow-up action is required, the regulating authority shall make every effort to prevent the disclosure of the reporter's name or that of any people engaged in the incident.

An approval must be granted by the Authority before dangerous goods can be carried on an aeroplane. Policy: The carriage of dangerous goods is strictly prohibited on all D0507 flights in accordance with NCO.OP.140. D0507 will not transport dangerous goods for any reason other than to do troubleshooting at our station or in the course of business operations. Accidents and occurrences are also possibilities for the particular carry. The list below are the example: Carry battery. A.24.7: Radio Communication between the Intercept Control Unit or the Intercepting Aircraft and the Intercepted Aircraft

When an interception is being made, the intercept control unit and the intercepting aircraft should: First attempt to establish two-way communication with the intercepted aircraft in a common language on the emergency frequency 121.5 MHz, using the call signs “INTERCEPT CONTROL”, “INTERCEPTOR (Call Sign)” and “INTERCEPTED AIRCRAFT” respectively. Failing this, attempt to establish two-way communication with the intercepted aircraft on such other frequency or frequencies as may have been prescribed by the appropriate ATS authority, or to establish contact through the appropriate ATS unit(s). If radio contact is established during interception but communication in a common language is not possible, attempts must be made to convey instructions, acknowledgement of instructions and essential information by using the standard phrases and pronunciations and transmitting each phrase twice.
A.24.8: Co-ordination between Intercept Control Units and Air Traffic Services Unit

It is essential that close co-ordination be maintained between an intercept control unit and the appropriate air traffic services unit during all phases of an interception of an aircraft which is, or might be, a civil aircraft, in order that the air traffic services unit is kept fully informed of the developments and of the action required if the intercepted aircraft.

B.1.1: D0507 is established to facilitate pilot training. By doing so, each operation is guided by the principles of safety, efficiency, and training quality. In order to meet the expectations of our trainee and/or the sponsor of the trainee, we strive to perform air operations to the greatest standard possible. It is imperative that all personnel possess a comprehensive understanding of the laws, rules, processes, and the information contained in this handbook that are pertinent to the execution of their responsibilities. They shall carry out their responsibilities in adherence to the pertinent laws, policies, regulations, directions, and procedures of D0507 and the states in which activities are carried out. Any deviation from or misuse of the operational safety standards of the company is considered a violation. Intentional transgressions of these norms could result in suitable repercussions.
B.1.2: The operations manual comprises methods and instructions that are derived from the most current technical data and operational experience. Their purpose is to organise and carry out training flights in adherence to the requirements set forth by the Thailand Air Navigation Act (THAI ANA) and D0507 policy. It is required that all Operations personnel adhere to every section of the Operations Manual.
B.1.3: D0507 must adhere to the specific provisions of the Authority-issued Approved Training Organization (ATO) (CAAT). In pursuit of this objective, D0507 should deploy competent staff and furnish the necessary tools and protocols to execute the specified duties with the intention of minimising inherent dangers. D0507 will regularly monitor its performance to identify inconsistencies at the earliest opportunity and shall take corrective steps to reinstate the desired standards upon discovery of a disparity.
B.1.4: Based upon THAI ANA regulations the CAAT will issue an ATO. To a certain amount national variant in terms of rules and regulations may still be in force. Having received an ATO, D0507 has satisfied the Authority that all provisions have been met to comply with the applicable regulatory framework. Furthermore, the ATO specifies the conditions under which the operating license has been issued. The D0507 Quality System assures the continuous compliance with the requirements of the ATO. Changes concerning authorized personnel, procedures or publications must only be put into effect after prior notification, if necessary prior approval of the CAAT. Failure to comply with the before mentioned provisions may have a negative effect on the ATO.

Procedure: Before the Flight (Pre-Flight Check): Pre-Flight Inspection: The Pilot in Command (PIC) or student must properly inspect the aircraft before takeoff using the approved checklist. Report any inspection faults or anomalies promptly. Reporting Defects: Defects must be logged in the Aircraft Technical Log and reported to the Flight Operations Department or Maintenance Team before the flight can proceed. Grounding the Aircraft: If the defect affects airworthiness, the aircraft must be grounded until the issue is resolved and cleared by the maintenance team. During the Flight (In-Flight Reporting): Detection of Defects: If any defect is noticed during the flight, the pilot must assess whether the issue affects the safety of the flight. Immediate action should be taken following emergency or non-normal procedures as per the aircraft manual. Reporting on Landing: Upon landing, the pilot must log the defect in the Aircraft Technical Log and inform the Flight Operations Department and Maintenance Team for immediate action. After t
B.5.4: Purpose: This procedure ensures that the aircraft is properly inspected, documented, and cleared for flight after undergoing any maintenance work. Procedure: Completion of Maintenance Work: Once maintenance or repairs have been completed, the Maintenance Team must perform a post-maintenance inspection to verify that the work has been carried out in accordance with regulatory standards and manufacturer recommendations. Aircraft Technical Log Update: The Aircraft Technical Log must be updated with details of the maintenance work performed, including parts replaced, systems tested, and any other relevant technical information. Aircraft Release Documentation: A Certificate of Release to Service (CRS) or sign form LAE or equivalent release document must be signed by an authorized maintenance technician or engineer, confirming that the aircraft is airworthy and safe for flight. The CRS must be entered into the Aircraft Technical Log, including the name, signature, and authorization details of the person releasing the aircraft. Final Verification: Before the aircraft is returned to service, the Flight Operations Department will cross-check the maintenance records and the release documents

The flight plan or mission should prioritize the minimum sector fuel for full tank refueling at an outstation over payload considerations. Economic outstation refueling should be prioritized, considering Maximum Takeoff Weight (MTOW) and Maximum Landing Weight (MLW). Uplifting only the required amount of block fuel is cost-effective, as carrying excess fuel for outstation refueling is not. C.2.4: When ATC issues a clearance and the pilot accepts it, this forms an agreement between ATC and the Pilot-in-Command (PIC) regarding the execution of the flight. This becomes the current flight plan, whether or not it matches the originally filed flight plan. If the PIC wants to change the flight plan after takeoff, they must request the change and get ATC's approval in the form of an amended clearance. Similarly, ATC can issue an amended clearance for traffic management. If the PIC and ATC cannot agree on the amended clearance, the flight will continue under the emergency authority of the PIC. Any request for an amended clearance should consider traffic and ATC's planning and coordination needs. A pilot should not accept a clearance if they cannot follow it safely or if it exceeds the aircraft's capabilities. The Pilot-in-Command (PIC) is ultimately responsible for the operation of the aircraft. An ATC clearance does not allow a pilot to break any regulations or to conduct unsafe operations. If severe weather requires an immediate change in course, the pilot can use their emergency authority. If a pilot does not understand a clearance or part of it, they should ask for clarification

No aircraft is to fly over any congested area of a city or town below 1,500 feet above the highest obstacle within a radius of 2,000 feet from the aircraft unless it is flying under instructions from ATC. Under IFR conditions, no aircraft is to fly lower than 1,000 feet above the highest fixed obstacle within 5 nautical miles of the aircraft. Exceptions are allowed only if necessary for takeoff or landing. Unless the aircraft is flying at or below 3,000 feet AMSL in VFR conditions and remains clear of cloud and in sight of the surface. C.4.1: Hot Season (March to May): High temperatures and dry conditions, particularly in central and northern Thailand. Rainy Season (June to October): High humidity, frequent heavy rain, and thunderstorms, with variations between the east and west coasts of the south. Cool Season (November to February): Mild to warm temperatures, lower humidity, and the most pleasant weather for travel, particularly in the north and central regions. From November to February, the weather in Thailand is influenced by several meteorological phenomena: Cold and Dry Air from the Asiatic High: Cold and dry air masses move from the Asiatic High over the warm South China Sea. This movement leads to the formation of small, sharply defined cumulus clouds with tops ranging between 4,000 and 6,000 feet. Fair weather generally prevails in regions including the South China Sea and the Philippines. Early Morning Fog: Early morning fog is common over Indochina, Thailand, and Burma during these months. Visibility is often restricted to less than two miles due to the fog. This fog typically starts three hours before sunrise and lasts until two hours after sunrise.
